text: hello everyone and welcome back to the deep dive it is so great to have you with us again it really
is glad to be here today we are going to do something a little bit different we're narrowing
our focus i mean usually we take on these huge sprawling topics right a whole book or a whole
historical period exactly but today we're putting one single chapter under the microscope resuming
all the way in and that's often where you find the most interesting stuff isn't it in the details to
really get the big picture sometimes you have to look at one small perfect part of it and our
focus today is just that we're looking exclusively at 2 samuel chapter 12. a chapter that is i mean
it's packed with drama it has everything it really does there's a powerful king a secret crime a
prophet with an incredibly dangerous message and a really tragic death but before we even get into
the story itself i want to start with an idea you brought up earlier the idea of a blind spot
well it kind of scares me a little it scares me too it's that thing from when you're driving
right exactly that you're in your car you check the rear view mirror you check the side mirror
everything looks clear you think the lane next to you is empty you're sure of it and
then you start to move over and suddenly a horn blares and you realize there's a huge truck
sitting right in that one spot you just can't see and that's where the crash happens it's terrifying
but we have those in life too don't we personality blind spots these flaws or you know mistakes in our
character that are obvious to everyone else crystal clear to them but we are completely
utterly blind to them it's a fundamental human problem i think psychologists go on about this all
the time we're almost always the last to know about our own worst habits and the king in our
story today king david he has a blind spot the size of a kingdom a really dangerous one so to
help us explore this we've got our usual stack of sources first of course is the biblical text
itself and for this deep dive we're using the
bible in basic english which i think is a fantastic choice for this chapter the language
is so direct and simple it really lets the power of the story shine through without getting bogged
down in you know archaic words it just cuts right to the bone yeah we also have some really sharp
literary analysis from robert alter his book the art of biblical narrative is just phenomenal oh
altar is essential for this kind of work he reads the bible not just as a sacred text but as a
masterpiece of literature he's always asking
why this word why is this detail here he helps you see the artistry the craft behind it all
he really does and rounding out our sources we have commentary from ellen g white
we're pulling from her books patriarchs and prophets and also the sda bible commentary
and she's so good at getting inside the character's head she gives us this incredible
perspective on the moral and uh the spiritual struggles they're going through their guilt
their fear and eventually their hope and just a quick note for all of you listening we know a lot
deep dive to practice your english which is amazing so we're going to make a real effort
today to give our vocabulary clear and simple simple words but deep ideas that's always the
goal that's the goal okay let's set the scene we're jumping right into 2 samuel chapter 12
but to really get it we need just a tiny bit of context what happened right before this chapter
opens well we have to be brief because our focus is chapter 12 but this context is absolutely crucial
king david who is you know the great hero of israel he's done something absolutely terrible
understatement of the year right he saw a woman bathsheba bathing he wanted her so he took her
even though she was married and she becomes pregnant she does and to cover up his sin
david arranges for her husband a loyal soldier named uriah to be sent to the front lines of a
battle and abandoned there so david isn't just an adulterer he's a murderer by proxy yes he used the
sword of the enemy to do his dirty work and the worst part is he thinks he's gotten away with it
that's the chilling part isn't it the silence time has passed uriah is dead and buried bathsheba has
mourned and is now officially david's wife the baby has been born and nobody is saying a word
well who could david is the king he has absolute power you don't just walk up to an ancient monarch
and accuse him of capital crimes that's a death sentence for you instantly so david is sitting
in the middle of the desert trying to convince himself that everything is fine now that he's
fixed it but ellen g white describes this period in such a powerful way she says that on the outside
david might have seemed fine but on the inside he was in agony his conscience was eating him
alive she uses this amazing image she says david was wrapped in his garments of sin garments of sin
i can't get that image out of my head it's like he's wearing his guilt like a heavy dark coat
that's it exactly imagine putting on a thick wool coat in the middle of a hot summer day it's
suffocating it's uncomfortable it makes you sweat and it covers you up it hides who you are and it
blinds you that's the key when you're so wrapped up in yourself in your own justifications and your
own lies you can't see anything clearly you're living in this self-imposed darkness okay so into
this darkness a man named nathan walks in the text says the lord sent him nathan is a prophet now
it's we often imagine these wild-eyed men shouting on a street corner repent right
but nathan doesn't shout no nathan is brilliant he's psychologically brilliant
he knows that a direct accusation will just hit david's wall of denial it'll trigger the defenses
david will get angry he'll justify himself or he'll just have nathan killed exactly so nathan
has to be clever he has to find a way to sneak past the guards of david's mind to get right into
that blind spot so he tells the story he tells the story this brings us right to the end of the
part 1 of our deep dive today the setup and the parable we're in verses 1 through 4. nathan comes
before the king and basically says your majesty i have a legal case i need you to be the judge
and this is the first part of his brilliant strategy he appeals to david's role to his
identity david isn't just a king he's the chief justice of israel he prides himself on his sense
of fairness he loves justice he's famous for it so this immediately gets his attention he's hooked he
wants to be the king of israel he wants to be the king of israel he wants to be the king of israel
